bibliographic_data.title: The Reasonable Effectiveness of Diverse Evaluation Data
bibliographic_data.abstract: In this paper, we present findings from an semi-experimental exploration of rater diversity and its influence on safety annotations of conversations generated by humans talking to a generative AI-chat bot. We find significant differences in judgments produced by raters from different geographic regions and annotation platforms, and correlate these perspectives with demographic sub-groups. Our work helps define best practices in model development—specifically human evaluation of generative models—on the backdrop of growing work on sociotechnical AI evaluations.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper explores how rater diversity impacts safety annotations in conversations generated by humans interacting with a generative AI-chatbot, highlighting significant differences and proposing methods for assessing these influences.
core_content.key_methodology: A large-scale study was conducted using 96 raters from two annotation platforms to evaluate 990 conversations across 24 safety characteristics. The experiment was repeated after four months with overlapping raters to measure consistency.
outcomes: Significant differences in judgments produced by raters from different geographic regions and annotation platforms were found.
Raters' backgrounds impact the labels they provide, contributing to potential biases in AI systems.
Discrepancies in rater consistency across two replications of the experiment were observed.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
In this paper, we present findings from an semi-experimental exploration of rater diversity and its influence on safety annotations of conversations generated by humans talking to a generative AI-chat bot. We find significant differences in judgments produced by raters from different geographic regions and annotation platforms, and correlate these perspectives with demographic sub-groups. Our work helps define best practices in model development—specifically human evaluation of generative models—on the backdrop of growing work on sociotechnical AI evaluations.

The overall research question guiding our study was: Do rater demographics help us improve reliability of annotations and ultimately provide guidance for collecting representative data for AI development and evaluation? The input data for this study was sampled from an 8K conversations dataset, with a sample of 990 conversations annotated by 40 unique raters per conversation. This annotation experiment was repeated with a different pool of 96 raters (including 19 overlapping raters) four months later.

Key outcomes include significant differences in judgments produced by raters from different geographic regions and annotation platforms, indicating that rater backgrounds impact the labels they provide. Discrepancies in rater consistency across two replications were also observed.
